changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "Lunch Takeout List 3"

TextENGNew: The collection of fables known in Japan as 'Aesop's Fables' is said to have been created by the ancient Greek fabulist Aesopus — written in English as 'Aesop.'
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: introduction to Aesop's Fables and their origin from the ancient Greek fabulist Aesopus.

TextENGNew: However, not all of them were Aesopus's own creations. The prevailing theory holds that many were folk fables that existed throughout Greece during his lifetime and afterward,
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: explanation that not all fables were Aesopus's original creations, many being pre-existing Greek folk tales.

TextENGNew: or folk tales passed down as moral teachings in Asia Minor, his homeland, which were later compiled under his name.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: continuation explaining folk tales from Asia Minor compiled under Aesop's name.

TextENGNew: Among the most famous of these tales is 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf' — also known as 'The Wolf and the Shepherd.'
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: identification of 'The Boy Who Cried Wolf' as one of the most famous fables.

TextENGNew: — A shepherd boy, bored with the monotony of his days, cries one day that a wolf has come. The adults hear his cries and rush over armed and in a panic, only to discover it was a lie.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: the opening of the Boy Who Cried Wolf story where the bored shepherd boy first raises a false alarm.

TextENGNew: The adults, exhausted from the fruitless ordeal, are furious. But the boy finds their reaction so entertaining that, to stave off boredom, he begins to raise the alarm again and again as the days go by.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: how the boy repeatedly cries wolf for entertainment, angering the adults.

TextENGNew: At first the adults come running each time, but eventually, fed up with the boy's lies, no one comes anymore.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: the adults eventually stop responding to the boy's false alarms.

TextENGNew: Then one day, a wolf truly does come to the pasture. The startled boy calls for help from the adults, but they assume it is another lie and ignore him. In the end, the sheep are eaten...
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: the climax where a real wolf comes but no one believes the boy, resulting in the sheep being eaten.

TextENGNew: The lesson of this tale as it is generally known is: 'Lying destroys trust.'
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: the commonly known moral of the fable — that lying destroys trust.

TextENGNew: ...And yet, when one considers the international circumstances of ancient Greece and Asia Minor at the time this fable was composed, an alternative interpretation has recently begun to emerge.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: introduction of an alternative interpretation of the fable based on historical context.

TextENGNew: Let us suppose, for example, that out of ten times the boy cries wolf, nine of them are lies.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: a hypothetical scenario — nine out of ten wolf cries being lies.

TextENGNew: In that case, is it truly the right course of action for the village adults to make no preparations for a wolf attack merely because the probability is ten percent?
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: the rhetorical question challenging whether ignoring a ten percent threat is wise.

TextENGNew: Looked at paradoxically: one of those ten times will inevitably be a real wolf.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: the paradoxical observation that one alarm out of ten is guaranteed to be real.

TextENGNew: That one real instance might be the tenth — or it might be the very first. And if the wolf truly comes on the first occasion, the probability is not ten percent at all: it is effectively one hundred percent.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: argument that the real wolf could come first, making the effective probability 100%.

TextENGNew: This holds true whether the total is ten times or a hundred.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: statement that this principle applies regardless of scale.

TextENGNew: Even if ninety-nine out of a hundred are lies, whether the one true alarm comes last or first makes an enormous difference in perceived probability and in consequence. And regrettably, in most cases, one only learns which it was after the outcome.
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: elaboration on how timing of the real event changes perceived probability, knowable only in hindsight.

TextENGNew: Even when the estimated probability is low, failing to prepare for the worst at all times means that the perceived probability and the resulting loss can shift dramatically. ...In other words, the lesson hidden within this fable may well have been: 'If you are prepared, you have nothing to fear.'
changeReason: Original translation was missing entirely; new translation provides: the alternative moral — that the fable's hidden lesson is about preparedness rather than just honesty.
